dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems.
Hi, my name is Maria, a paramedic with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I can't breathe well, and it's been getting worse. Started about an hour ago.
Can you open your eyes for me?
How are you today?
Can you squeeze my fingers to show me your strength?
We're going to do a few things now. First, can we take a look at your breathing?
I'm going to check your breathing sounds. Can I listen?
Let me take your pulse and see how you're doing.
Do you have a history of asthma or breathing problems?
Are you currently using any medications to help with your breathing?
What were you doing when this started to feel worse?
Would you say your breathing feels like it's tight or wheezy, or something else?
Let's look at your skin for anything unusual. Does it feel warm or cool?
I'm going to shine a light in your eyes. It won't hurt.
Is there a specific area you're wheezing more in, right or side?
I won't press hard, but let me feel your belly for tenderness.
Can I feel your legs for any swelling or issues?
Your oxygen level is 96%.
We're going to put on a high-flow oxygen mask.
Applying a nebulizer to help open your airways now.
Albuterol and Atrovent have been given through the nebulizer.
We're following protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress-asthma exacerbation.
Your pulse is now 90.
Your breathing rate is now 14.
Blood pressure is stable at 112/80.
Oxygen is now up to 100%.
Your head, eyes, nose, and throat appear normal. No signs of trauma in these areas.
Your trachea is midline and supple, no deviation or breathing difficulty noted there.
Your lung sounds are currently very occluded, mostly wheezing in both left and right.
I see in your history that you've previously been intubated. Is that correct?
Your pelvis is intact and shows no signs of instability or pain.
Your glucose is 0 mg/dl, and EKG showed sinus rhythm earlier.
Your abdomen is normal with no tenderness, rigidity, or guarding found upon assessment.
I don't see any signs of jugular venous distension on examination.
Peripheral movement and sensation appear intact in all extremities during our initial checks.
Your airway is patent and maintainable. You seem able to manage it well.
I see you tried your Advair inhaler before calling 911. That's correct, right?
I don't see signs of insect bites or stings on your body based on the skin exam completed.
You don't have any known allergies listed. Is there anything you want to confirm about that?
You're on Baclofen and Ventolin now, if I've read your records correctly.
Skin has no new rash or bruising, no signs of recent trauma found on inspection.
What made the breathing symptoms worse just before calling 911?
You seem to be responding well to verbal commands-no signs of hearing loss observed.
When was your last meal or drink before the symptoms started?
Your initial breathing rate was 18, now it's better at 14 with treatment.
Did you take your Ventolin before calling 911? That's part of your usual dosage?
Your leg pulses are good-nothing concerning there upon palpation now.
Do you still feel short of breath, or is that better now post-treatment?
Your chest is normal in shape and contour, without signs of recent injury detected on visual inspection.
You respond to auditory cues appropriately, indicating no acute hearing loss detected.
No signs of nystagmus or abnormal eye movements observed during the exam.
No lateralizing neurological deficits or field defects found during assessment today.
Your eyes show no corneal lesions or redness during current examination.
No flank or back pain reported and none detected during assessment.
No signs of swelling in the neck area or thyroid enlargement detected during physical exam.
Your chest is normal in shape and contour, without signs of injury on visual and palpation examination.
Motor function is appropriate with responses as expected from initial testing today. Removed reference to restraints and sensation not grounded in ePCR.
Pain assessments show a current score of 0 with no significant complaints for now.
We continue to follow respiratory distress protocol. Ready for transport to [COMPANY_NAME] with ongoing monitoring.
Ready for transport in a secured position with continuous monitoring setup.
Confirming your BP is still 112/80, pulse 90, and O2 is 100% at current time.
You'll be taken to Trauma Bed 8 at [COMPANY_NAME] with all necessary critical care prepared.
The hospital has been notified for Trauma Bed 8 with a trauma team on standby.
Arrival is scheduled, and the hospital has all the details for their emergency team.
The transport team will depart promptly at a safe speed to maintain vitals during transit.
Transport departs at 11:36 as noted to ensure a timely and safe journey.
No major issues or interventions occurred during the transport process.
Upon arrival at [COMPANY_NAME], emergency care will begin in the designated trauma bed.
You remain within stable and safe levels on monitors, no major concerns.
Nebulizer therapy and oxygen are ongoing as per our standing treatment orders.
4 lead monitoring continues for full clinical documentation during travel.
Vital signs and breathing show improvement as noted from start of transport.
Because of your weight of 98 kg, we have coordinated with the hospital for proper bed support.
All equipment and monitoring are in place to ensure your transport safety.
We've documented all procedures and medications as part of our EPCR for transmission.
Your latest BP is confirmed at 112/80-no signs of distress recorded.
High flow oxygen remains at 100% continuously during transport as needed.
Albuterol and Atrovent have been fully administered and documented in our system.
Updates will continue to you on board during the journey to keep you informed.
Transport has begun with restraints ready to ensure full safety and comfort.
All final vitals and EKG data will be sent to the receiving hospital for their records.
Unfortunately, due to decreased LOC, no signatures were obtained from you for records.
The receiving hospital care team has been alerted about your decreased LOC for arrival.
